parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: They told me to just relax and stop overthinking everything, like it’s some simple switch I can flip. I tried to laugh it off, but the comment just sat heavy in my chest all afternoon. I keep replaying the moment in my head and wondering why I always seem to make simple moments so complicated. It feels like I’m constantly tripping over my own words and leaving everyone to clean up the mess. I just wish I could handle things without turning them into such a heavy ordeal.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry that you're feeling this way, but I'm here to talk if you need. It's important to remember that everyone processes things differently, and what might seem simple to one person can feel overwhelming to another. It's not a reflection of your strength or intelligence, but rather a part of what makes you unique.

It's also important to note that telling someone to "just relax" or "stop overthinking" can often feel dismissive or invalidating, even if that's not the intention. It's okay to take time to process your thoughts and feelings, and it's okay to ask for help or support when you need it.

Instead of trying to "flip a switch," maybe it would help to focus on small steps or strategies that can help you manage overwhelming feelings or situations. This could be anything from taking deep breaths, practicing mindfulness, or even just taking a break when you need it.

Remember, it's okay to have moments where you feel like you're not handling things perfectly. It's a part of being human. What's important is that you're aware of your feelings and are taking steps to understand and manage them.

If these feelings persist or start to interfere with your daily life, it may be helpful to speak with a mental health professional. They can provide you with strategies and tools tailored to your specific needs.

In the meantime, be kind to yourself. You're doing the best you can, and that's enough. It's okay to have moments of doubt or insecurity, but try not to let them define you. You're more than your thoughts and feelings in any given moment.

turns.user_message: I keep noticing how their tone shifts whenever I start talking, and it makes me want to shrink into myself completely. It’s been happening for months now, and I can’t shake the feeling that I’m the common denominator in every awkward silence. I try to keep my thoughts to myself, but they always spill out anyway, usually at the worst possible times. I end up apologizing constantly, even when nobody actually says anything is wrong. The weight of it just keeps building, and I hate how predictable my own reactions have become.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry that you're feeling this way, but I'm here to help. It's completely normal to feel this way, but it's not healthy to keep these feelings bottled up. It might be helpful to try and communicate your feelings to the person or people you're experiencing this with. Let them know how their tone shifts make you feel. It's possible that they might not even realize they're doing it, or that it's affecting you this way.

It's also important to remember that you shouldn't have to apologize for expressing your thoughts and feelings. It's okay to have moments of awkward silence, and it's okay if you're not always the most eloquent or articulate person in the room. Everyone has their own unique way of communicating, and that's what makes us human.

If you find that these feelings are overwhelming and affecting your daily life, it might be helpful to speak to a mental health professional. They can provide you with strategies and tools to help you manage these feelings and improve your communication skills.

In the meantime, try to be kind to yourself. It's okay to have off days, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed. You're not alone in this, and there are people who care about you and want to support you. Keep taking small steps forward, and don't be too hard on yourself if you have setbacks. You're doing the best you can, and that's enough.

turns.user_message: Every single time they sigh or glance away, I take it as proof that I’m fundamentally exhausting to be around. I’ve convinced myself that my presence is just a constant drain on everyone else’s energy, and I can’t stop punishing myself for it. I replay every conversation looking for the exact moment I ruined the mood, and the list just keeps growing longer. I feel completely trapped inside my own head, watching myself push people away while pretending everything is fine. It’s exhausting to carry this heavy guilt around, knowing I’m probably the reason the room feels so tense.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry that you're feeling this way, but I want to assure you that your feelings are not facts. It's common for people to have moments of self-doubt or insecurity, but it's important not to let them spiral into a persistent negative narrative about yourself.

Firstly, it's crucial to understand that you can't read minds. Just because someone sighs or looks away, it doesn't necessarily mean it's because of you or that you're exhausting to be around. People have a wide range of reasons for their actions or expressions, and it's not fair to you to assume the worst.

Secondly, it's important to challenge these thoughts. Ask yourself: "Would I say this to a friend who was feeling this way?" If not, why are you saying it to yourself? Try to be as kind and understanding to yourself as you would be to someone else.

Lastly, consider speaking to a mental health professional. They can provide strategies and tools to help you manage these feelings and thoughts. You don't have to carry this burden alone.

In the meantime, try to focus on the positive aspects of your interactions. There are likely many moments where you've made others laugh, feel understood, or enjoy your company. Remember, everyone has value and brings something unique to the table. You are no exception.

It's also important to communicate openly with the people around you. If you're feeling this way, it might be helpful to express your feelings to them. They might not realize the impact of their actions and could provide reassurance.

Lastly, be patient with yourself. Changing thought patterns takes time and practice. But with each small step, you're moving towards a healthier and happier mindset. You deserve to live a life free from this heavy guilt.
